content.text: JSX attributes look like HTML attributes but they work differently under the hood. In HTML, attribute values are always strings. In JSX, attribute values can be strings, JavaScript expressions, objects, arrays, or functions — and the syntax you use signals which type you mean.

attrs.alt: JSX attribute types: string literals, dynamic expressions, boolean shorthand, className patterns, and style objects

content.text: Three Ways to Pass an Attribute Value
When the value is a static string known at write time, use quotes:
Link
When the value comes from a variable, function call, or computation, use curly braces (no quotes):
Never mix quotes and curly braces for the same value.
A bare attribute name without a value is shorthand for

<button disabled>Can't click</button>     // disabled={true}
<button disabled={true}>Can't click</button>  // identical
<button>Can click</button>                // disabled={false}

<input required />
<input readOnly />
is a reserved keyword in JavaScript.

// Multiple classes in one string (same as HTML)
<div className="card card-elevated shadow-md">
Dynamic class names with template literals
Adding or removing a class based on state is one of the most common patterns in React:
(with a trailing space). That's harmless — browsers ignore extra whitespace in class names — but it's slightly untidy. The
) joins class strings while skipping falsy values:
, so conditional classes never leave empty strings or trailing spaces.
takes a CSS string. In JSX, it takes a JavaScript object with camelCase property names:
React converts numeric property values to pixel strings for CSS length properties. Non-length properties (like
) take numbers as-is. For units other than

<div style={{
  width: 300,         // → '300px'
  padding: 16,        // → '16px'
  fontSize: '1.5rem', // string — explicit unit
  opacity: 0.8,       // number, no unit appended
  zIndex: 10,         // number, no unit appended
}}>
Vendor prefixes are PascalCase in JSX style objects:

<div style={{
  WebkitUserSelect: 'none',  // -webkit-user-select
  MozUserSelect: 'none',     // -moz-user-select
  msUserSelect: 'none',      // -ms-user-select (ms is lowercase)
}}>
is a JS reserved word. JSX uses
Event handler attribute names are camelCase —
. Their values are function references or arrow functions (not strings):
Pass a function, don't call it:
calls it immediately during render — a common mistake.
Custom data attributes and ARIA attributes keep their hyphenated form in JSX — they're the exception to the camelCase rule:

// data-* attributes — keep hyphens
<button data-testid="submit-btn" data-action="submit">
  Submit
</button>

// aria-* attributes — keep hyphens
<button
  aria-label="Close dialog"
  aria-expanded={isOpen}
  aria-controls="menu-list"
>
  Menu
</button>

// role is not hyphenated
<div role="dialog" aria-modal={true}>
When building wrapper components, you often want to pass through unknown attributes to the underlying DOM element. The spread operator collects them:
CSS Modules scope class names to a file by hashing them at build time. You import the module as an object and reference class names as properties:
— globally unique, no collisions across files.
Tailwind's utility classes are applied via
just like any CSS class. The pattern of computing class strings based on props is central to building Tailwind component libraries:
only for values that cannot be expressed as classes:
Values computed at runtime (% widths, user colours)
HTML inputs manage their own value internally. In React, you typically control inputs by wiring
to update that state — a pattern called a controlled component:
, the input always reflects React state. Without it, the input is uncontrolled — React won't know what the user typed.
React passes unknown attributes to the DOM as-is if they are lowercase. Camelcase unknown attributes trigger a warning in the console. This matters when building wrapper components that spread props:
Computed Attributes in Practice: A Card Component
